sentences.text: Yes.
It started before the war, this friendship.
And especially [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for us, it was also very nice because we, we were in the same age as children of Melamed family.
I remember them very well.
They came sometimes to us for-- especially for Easter.
Or we went to-- for Passover.
I never forget this terrific [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cake, what was the-- I forgot, sorry.
It was very-- I can tell that it was very close friendship.
And especially when they need help, my parents was a really good friend.
When all Jews were-- had to be in ghetto.
My father, because of business, he has permission to go there.
And he took, sometimes, food or something.
They-- he help Melameds.
And he help-- I don't know really how.
Because really, I was not allow-- allowed to, to be and to know too much.
I was too young.
They left ghetto.
And my father and my brother helped them to hide and to leave Warsaw.
Yes.
I remember also family Neufeld.
This man was without one arm.
I don't remember why.
But this family I remember because in-- during occupation when Jewish were hiding, they wanted to give [INAUDIBLE] to safe place.
And I saw them in our apartment.
They, they were talking with my parents.
And this boy was separated for parent and given, given under help of our Catholic friend, friend.
This is family Neufeld, and I know also they lost life in Treblinka or Majdanek.
I don't remember where.
But Melamed were safe.
I can't tell you about another names.
Mostly, my brother who was eight years older as I was, he, he was-- he took place with my father to help.
You see, just before the war, my parents knew that something could happen.
It was very-- everybody was very excited.
And Polish government was concentrate army.
Because my mother was in first war with father, they knew that it's necessary to concentrate food.
They, they, they was very good prepared.
We had a lot of food, milk in powder.
And also, many people together with us in the basement.
But when war start to begin, 1st September, 199-- '39, it was still suddenly and very cruel-- bombarding, bombarding, and bombarding.
Everybody tried to save life sitting in basement.
But anyway, so many men was in the front.
My brother was also.
And our family gave shelter for many people.
It was shocking for me.
I tell you, I was probably very stupid.
I was very curious.
How could be-- how it looks war?
Because my parents very often talk about war in 194-- '14 And I was very curious.
Until I was shocked how it looks.
I saw even this moment when castle was bombard and the, the big place in fire and falling down.
It was really shocking for me.
Yes.
Where were you when you saw the, the bombings?
War start-- war start when I was on [INAUDIBLE], close to, to [INAUDIBLE].
But my father, supposed that is very good to save us, took us to embassy where he had a lot of friends because it was part in Warsaw where street for embassies.
And he suppose it will be not bomb-- bombing there.
For a couple days, really, it was quiet.
But we return home.
And our house was half destroyed-- but still, everybody alive.
I remember my mother, who is seeing soldiers, Polish soldiers, very depressed because they knew that this too big army, German army for-- against Polish army.
And she took everything what she had in the store, giving the soldier.
It was starting.
Because I had-- I asked her, why you doing?
You have to know if you share something, it returns to you or to your children.
Attitude.
Which embassy did you go to?
It was Estonian, I think.
For me, I, I felt that it was finish of our freedom.
Everybody was depressed, crying.
Immediately, we felt that they want to destroy our country and people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First, they close all school.
And they let also only to have trade school, not to have any intelligentsia.
And since occupation, on the beginning he were looking for people who was against German.
Also, arresting many professors, doctors, lawyers without reason, only to kill intelligentsia.
To make-- Their goal was to make slave from Polish people.
Very bad-- I, I share with my family these sad days.
Everybody was involved in political news.
Or we-- we were very involved in, in it.
Everybody asking.
At first, it was no food.
We have to fight to get some piece of bread.
I know that my mother had a lot of food.
But still, bread, it was unable to get.
For a couple, couple days, it was very hard, but later, worse.
Then my brother was in the army.
Paul, the, the oldest, was somewhere close on northeast.
And we were very, very unhappy, because we didn't have nothing about him.
And also, shocking for all of us, was news that 17 days later when Ger-- German came to Poland, Russian broke from West and start to fight like enemies.
It was very, very upsetting us.
Nobody supposed it could be.
17 Of September-- they, they came without warning, sending people to Siberia or killing people without any reason because they are Polish [INAUDIBLE].
This mean ?] not simple people, but some special.
I don't know why it was so bad attitude.
But since centuries, we know that we are between two giants.
1939, the same-- 17 days after German army attacked Poland, only 17 days.
I don't remember exactly when he reopened something, when he start to work.
Because he was energetic person and hard-working.
How they survived these first days, I don't remember.
Only I remember that my school start to be in-- this building was evacuated because command-- German commander took it, nice building.
And we start to have classes in different small houses-- to-- our teachers continue this teaching work.
But it was forbidden to have geography and history, only German language.
It was, of course, necessary.
And you see, slowly, but we have underground education concerning history and geography.
I remember my teacher-- oh, it was nice person.
And I finish seven, seven grade in-- during occupation.
But was very difficult because always was German looking for people to arrest and take to, to working camp or to concentration camp.
Life in this time-- I was in Warsaw.
In this time was very hard.
Even if you took tram, overcrowded.
But you never know if a soldier will stop them, [GERMAN].
And they took for killing, or to concentration, or to working camp-- very unsafe life.
Food was also very, very poor.
Usually, we had [INAUDIBLE] small and was couple, couple marmalade from beets.
No-- everybo-- everything what we can eat, it was necessary to buy on private places.
The people brought to Warsaw many food.
But it was very difficult to, to get that.
All-- yeah, I remember that this was time when I was growing fast.
Between 11 and 16, I was in Warsaw.
Communication was not safe.
Because always, you can suspect something.
I saw a couple times going on the street, and immediately, two or three gendarmerie came to one person, took them to the big car.
And he disappeared.
And later, many people were shot on the street in special place on spot.
Next day, the same places was covered with lights and flowers.
We have still the same now in Warsaw.
In a couple places where Polish-- it was not soldier.
I don't know.
Because of people, they shot them.
Yes.
Therefore, each day, mother was praying, oh, God, let me see my children.
Seven o'clock was time where nobody should be on the street.
And she was waiting till seven o'clock.
Oh, God, let me be with my children-- hard time.
My brother, George, was much earlier as I was in underground army, he had-- he passed special preparation for, to, for shooting and everything.
He was in underground army, I think, since 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also, going to this underground ar-- underground school, where he was very active in preparing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] theater, school theater.
He was very, very dedicated to painting.
But you see, in this time nobody knows what you doing.
Because it was secret.
I remember when I gave my oath, I said, never tell about where I am, what I'm doing, what I have to do.
It was secret.
I was 15 years old.
My girlfriend, very close friend from school, Mischka, she has connection with officer from underground army.
And they tried to, to prepare young girls for first aids for soldier, wounded soldier, and later, to have-- to, to learn how to work with weapons, with pistols.
And this-- it started-- I remember it started very early or very early 1944, these courses.
It was only group of three girls.
And one older person teach us first aid.
Yes.
It was especially-- we, we knew what's happened in ghetto.
Everybody was very suffering.
People I know that helped them a lot, especially [INAUDIBLE] organization.
My hu-- My father was helping.
But so many people could.
But late as [? fall, ?] it was more difficult even to do something for them, especially when April, 1943, ghetto started to be finished.
And was uprising in ghetto.
It was like hell.
I can't go to ghetto.
I could pass only close to ghetto with tram, which was going close.
And I saw [? sick-- ?] unbelievable.
People crying, jumping from windows, burning, smell of burning corpse-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how you can help?
I understood.
Because we had open heart for everybody, and special knowing that there's so many people, children, old people.
And my father told me-- he was crying coming from ghetto when he told us it was devastating.
You see, special-- I know this Melamed family.
And I forgot to tell you during this time, I was visiting this Neufeld boy, which was hidden in our friend house, a boy, a Jewish boy.
I forget the name-- first name.
He was hidden behind the closet in very small room.
And our friend, Mr.
Sigmund, he gave him only what he had-- food and, and [? security. ?] And this boy in my age, he knew all [? and security, ?] all.
I was shocked.
I could [? sit. ?]
I was invited why?
Because they knew that I'm a secret person.
And I'm in the same age.

And this boy has to talk with somebody, because he could be like animal [? closed. ?] And you see for me, [? news-- ?] when I returned to Poland, to Poland again from concentration camp that he was shot.
It was very bad.
How old were you when you went to visit him?
14, 15-- let me see-- 15 years old.
He was maybe in my age, maybe younger.
Why?
This is quite normal.
If somebody ask and we know that what will happen to Jewish, this is normal to help other people.
This is human, same as us.
But we can't take to our home.
Because with four children and father could-- you know what was-- if somebody help Jewish people, could be killed immediately on the spot.
Therefore, this single man, he decide to help them.
No.
Sigmund Tiszlikowski, very nice man.
And I heard that he was shot during uprising in Warsaw.
Because when it was bombarded, destroyed all houses.
They had to go to basements.
I don't know really how.
But I know that he and this boy was shot by German soldier.
Yeah.
Because of this business.
He had some connection with still with ghetto specialist in factories.
I can't tell you why.
But he has permission because connection was business.
Oh, maybe a couple-- once a week.
I don't remember.
Only he was shocked and crying sometimes, talking what happened, what he saw.
He took always something with him for food.
You know, that always is-- somebody can help a little.
But so many people was there, dying on the street.
Very crowded.
People-- you see, from the tram, it was very difficult to see.
But we observe.
And we heard from free Europe radio, which was, of course, forbidden.
But we knew so much.
But we can't help.
We-- many, many children were taken from ghetto.
But not all parents wanted to give children.
You understand them, no?
Because they suppose that better be together.
But many children were taking to, to priest, to sister, non-sister.
And they survived.
But these people who stay there, no.
I can't tell you this because was all the time to Warsaw ghetto, they brought from all Europe Jewish people, from all, all Europe.
In another towns, also they brought people from outside.
But I can't tell you.
Probably nobody knows how many.
And special [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] me, because I was still too young.

Only I had very bad memory about my friend from my school who wanted to the group of a small sold-- Polish soldier from underground army, who wanted to open the wall to ghetto and to withdraw as much as possible Jewish people to Aryan side.
But it was-- this action was not successful.
And he lost life.
Also, very, very big-- it was first and the best friend for my brother and for me.
Yes.
We, we knew about Kristallnacht.
And it was before the war, of course.
OK.
